title: The Whole Christ
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Whole Christ - Pastor Tito Lyro - 1 John 1_1-4.txt
outline: Introduction: 1 John 1:1-4 — the prologue to the epistle
That which was from the beginning: the eternal Word
The apostolic witness: heard, seen, and handled
The manifestation of eternal life in the Word made flesh
Fellowship with the Father and the Son
The fullness of joy in fellowship with Christ
Conclusion: The whole Christ — fully God, fully man, fully ours
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches from 1 John 1:1-4 on the whole Christ as presented in John's prologue. The apostle testifies to the reality of the incarnate Word of life — that which was heard, seen, and handled — inviting believers into fellowship with the Father and Son and the fullness of joy.
transcript:
Christianity is either the whole Christ — fully divine, fully human — or nothing. There is nothing in between. We either are believers in the Lord Jesus Christ, or we are not Christians. We have a system of doctrines, we have traditions that we follow. But at the end of the day, Christianity is Jesus Christ. Take Jesus out of it and you no longer have Christianity. Take the human or the divine out of it, and you have some other religion — not the religion of the Bible.

Brothers and sisters, I hope we understand that the God-man Jesus Christ is of the essence of who we are as Christians. If it doesn't exist — or if it is anything less than the fully divine and fully human together — we are, of all people, the most miserable. We are to be pitied, because we follow a false God, a God that cannot do anything for us.

That is the point that John makes throughout this little letter, and he makes it specifically in these four verses. These four verses are really about the two natures of Christ — about the union of the divine and the human into one person. In theological terms, these verses are about the hypostatic union of the person of Christ. That may sound like something you would read when you cannot sleep, but that is not the case. Stay with me. I want to show you how incredibly practical this doctrine is — how life-giving it is that God revealed himself in the flesh in the Lord Jesus Christ.

The hypostatic union of Christ is, to me, the most difficult doctrine in all of Christian theology to wrap my mind around: how in one person you can have the divine and the human, united forever without being separated, without confusion of the two natures, without one nature bleeding into the other — and that being a forever existence from the moment of conception on. It is utterly essential to our faith, and it is utterly practical.

## Background: Why John Wrote

First John is a short epistle — only five chapters, 105 verses, about 2,400 words in the original language. John wrote it in the early AD 90s, likely to the church in Ephesus or to the churches in that neighborhood — the Lycus Valley churches, the seven churches listed in Revelation, most of which were probably started by the Ephesian church. By this time, John was the pastor in Ephesus, having likely taken over from Timothy.

The primary reason he wrote was that John became aware of false teachers who had come into the church and were threatening her well-being. These teachers were saying two things: first, that the incarnation of God the Son never happened — that Jesus the Christ never became human as we are. And second, that faith in God does not require obedience — that you can have true faith in God and live a life that does not reflect that faith. To answer these issues, John writes 1 John.

## A Look at the Text: The Dirty Dishes

I want to go through the first four verses in a general way, make some comments about the passage, and then come back and apply them. In order to understand what is going on, we do have to dig a little bit into the grammar. Someone told me that when you are preaching a sermon, it is like cooking a meal. You present a beautiful meal to your guests, but you never show them the dirty pans — you leave them in the kitchen. The dirty pans are your Greek and Hebrew. Well, we are going to take a look at a few dirty dishes this morning so that we can set the table for the rest of the epistle.

Notice that John starts this book abruptly. There is no greeting. There is no introduction. He just starts teaching right away. Some suggest that 2 John was actually a cover letter to 1 John, but more likely this letter starts so abruptly because John was so concerned with the well-being of his beloved church that he did not want to waste any time on introductory matters.

This is a letter unlike any other letter in the New Testament. The only other letter that starts this way is Hebrews, which is more of a sermon than a natural letter. And these four verses are the nightmare of English teachers because they form one sentence in the original language — just one sentence with several subordinate clauses.

Notice also that verse 2 in our translation is a parenthetical comment. It is not part of the main argument — signified by the two dashes, one at the beginning and one at the end. You could remove verse 2 and still have a cohesive argument from verses 1, 3, and 4. But verse 2 is also important, as we will see.

I want you to notice all the "which" clauses. There are four of them: *which was from the beginning; which we have heard; which we have seen; which we have looked upon; which our hands have handled* (verse 1) and *which we have seen and heard* (verse 3). All of these clauses are dependent on the word "declare" in verse 3. That word governs everything else. John Calvin suggests this preface be summarized as follows: *We declare to you the word of life, which was from the beginning and was really testified to us in all manner of ways, that life has appeared in him.* The declaring is the main thing John wants to draw our attention to.

The word translated "declare" in our version means *to proclaim by commission* — not just a simple saying of things, but proclaiming something when you are commissioned to do so. John is speaking with Christ's authority as a commissioned apostle. Christ said that his apostles would proclaim his word in his authority. You see this in Acts 1:8:

> *"You shall receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you, and you shall be witnesses to Me in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth."*

So when John writes 1 John, he is not merely suggesting things to us, not throwing out ideas. He is authoritatively proclaiming that Jesus is the Christ — that the divine and the human have become one in Jesus Christ — by the authority that Christ gave to him.

John and the other apostles were compelled to proclaim the gospel of Christ, the whole Christ. Remember when Peter and John were arrested in Acts 4 and were told not to proclaim Christ anymore:

> *"Whether it is right in the sight of God to listen to you more than to God, you judge. For we cannot but speak the things which we have heard and seen."*

They had the same compulsion to preach as Jeremiah, who said when he tried to stop:

> *"His word was in my heart like a burning fire shut up in my bones; I was weary of holding it back, and I could not."*

That burning, that compulsion, is what we mean when we speak of the apostolic proclamation. And John has two objectives in writing this preface. Look at verses 3 and 4. He says he declares these things *"that you also may have fellowship with us"* — that is the first objective. And then in verse 4: *"these things we write to you that your joy may be full"* — that is the second. He is writing so that Christians may have fellowship with each other and with the Trinity, and so that Christians may have joy.

## The Dirty Dishes Back in the Kitchen

Now let us see what this means and apply it.

### Jesus Is Fully Divine

The first thing I want to show you is that John proclaims a Jesus who is divine — affirmed by God himself. Notice that this letter starts very similarly to the Gospel of John, which makes sense — the same author wrote both. Remember how the Gospel of John begins:

> *"In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. He was in the beginning with God."*

Now notice in verse 1 of 1 John that the word of life has existed *from the beginning.* If something was there in the beginning, it must have existed prior to the beginning. If you are already there when the beginning happens, you have to exist prior to it — that is to say, you exist without a beginning. The word of life existed eternally.

John makes this clearer in chapter 2, verses 13 and 14: *"I write to you, fathers, because you have known Him who is from the beginning."* So here we have the word of life primarily referring to the Apostolic Proclamation — the words that give life, the gospel of salvation — united with the person of Jesus Christ himself, existing forever, eternally, before the beginning.

This word of life is personified as eternal life that was with the Father. Look at verse 2:

> *"The life was manifested and we have seen, and bear witness, and declare to you that eternal life which was with the Father and was manifested to us."*

Eternal life is a person, and his name is Jesus Christ. He was with the Father forever, because he is God himself. As God the Son, he was with the Father before the incarnation, and he continues to be with the Father. In John 17:1–5, Jesus prays that the glory he had from all eternity with the Father would be restored, and indeed it was. So John teaches in these few verses that Jesus is fully divine.

### Jesus Is Fully Human

John also teaches that Jesus Christ is fully human. As you would expect, John spends more time on the humanity of Jesus than on his deity, since the humanity was what was under attack. The early church had more issues with Jesus being a man than with him being God — which is the reverse of today. Most people are willing to say Jesus was a good man; what bothers them is the idea that he was also divine.

Notice how John appeals to his physical senses to show the church that Jesus was a real human:

- *"We have heard"* (verse 1 and 3) — and the way it is written describes the vividness of that hearing. Sixty-plus years later, the voice of Jesus was still ringing in their ears.
- *"We have seen with our eyes"* (verse 1) — not figuratively seen with the mind, but with actual eyeballs.
- *"We have looked upon"* (verse 1) — "to look upon" means to see with intelligence, to understand what one is seeing. They did not merely observe; they comprehended what they were seeing.
- *"Our hands have handled"* (verse 1) — this is likely a reference to the events after the resurrection. Remember that on the first Sunday, Jesus appeared in the upper room and said, *"Handle me and see, for a spirit does not have flesh and bones as you see I have"* (Luke 24:39). To prove it, he ate food before them. John is clear that the Jesus he knew was flesh and blood — not an apparition, not a ghost, but a real flesh-and-blood human being.

Note also that John keeps using "we" throughout the passage. He is not giving his private testimony — he is speaking on behalf of the apostles, relating to us the collective witness of the apostles.

### Verse 2: Both Natures Together

Verse 2 brings both natures together:

> *"The life was manifested and we have seen, and bear witness, and declare to you that eternal life which was with the Father and was manifested to us."*

That which was from the beginning — that eternal, divine Word — could be seen, heard, and touched because he himself made himself manifest: he appeared. He initiated it. He manifested himself in the flesh to his people. As John says in the Gospel, *"The Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld His glory."* John Stott says in his commentary on 1 John: *"We could not have seen the one who was eternally with the Father unless he had taken the initiative deliberately to manifest himself. Human beings can apprehend only what God is pleased to make known."*

### Why Does This Doctrine Matter?

Why does this mighty doctrine — with its complicated name, the hypostatic union — matter? First, because the Bible teaches it. But beyond that, there are vital theological implications.

Jesus Christ your Savior must be human in order to obey God's law as a human. He came to do what Adam could not do — as our representative, to perfectly obey every requirement of the law of God for us. And in order to stand in our place, he had to be just like we are. Paul teaches this in Romans 5:18–19:

> *"Therefore, as through one man's offense judgment came to all men, resulting in condemnation, even so through one Man's righteous act the free gift came to all men, resulting in justification of life."*

The righteousness that Jesus Christ earned was human righteousness, so that when we believe in him, that human righteousness is counted as ours. A human also needed to pay for the sins of humans on the cross — so Jesus Christ had to be fully human, otherwise he is no Savior at all.

But Jesus Christ must also be fully God in order to bear the infinite punishment for the sins of the elect in a finite amount of time. He was crucified for only a few hours. And in those hours, he bore the infinite wrath of God due to our sin. In order to do that, he had to be fully God, because finite beings cannot bear infinity in a limited space of time. He had to be both at the same time — otherwise he cannot save you.

## The Results: Fellowship and Joy

Sometimes we think that these deep doctrines are a waste of time. We think we would be better served by a sermon on how to raise our children, or how to resolve our conflicts, or how to get along with our in-laws. But remember two things when that temptation comes.

First, this life and its relationships and blessings are not all there is — they are not even the most important thing. Satan would be perfectly content if all we cared about was living the best, most moral life now. Jesus himself says, *"Do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul. But rather fear Him who is able to destroy both soul and body in hell."*

Second, this life is better lived in faith in the person and the work of the Lord Jesus Christ. The whole Christ — the God-man — is the one who opens the door to fullness of life both now and forevermore.

### Fellowship

Faith in the whole Christ brings you into fellowship. Look at the end of verse 3:

> *"And truly our fellowship is with the Father and with His Son Jesus Christ."*

Faith in the whole Christ brings you into fellowship with the triune God. The false teachers were saying that you could have fellowship with God apart from the whole Christ — you do not need the human Jesus, just buy the teaching I have to sell you. But the only way to be in fellowship with the true God of the Bible is through the whole Christ and his redeeming work, accomplished as the God-man.

Fellowship with the triune God also means fellowship with the apostles. Look at the beginning of verse 3: *"That which we have seen and heard we declare to you, that you also may have fellowship with us."* The "us" refers to the apostles who are witnesses of the person and work of the Lord Jesus Christ. We have fellowship with the apostles by believing in what they proclaimed about the person and work of Jesus Christ. And because of that fellowship with the apostles, we have fellowship with the church throughout the ages through our common faith.

We sang two hymns this morning that are 1,000 and 1,700 years old. We are united with the church through the ages because we believe in the Christ who is fully God and fully man in one person. We have fellowship with the church throughout the world today for the same reason. A brother sent me a video of his congregation's worship this morning, singing a song about the same Christ we are singing about right here today.

But this is where the rubber meets the road: all of these connections are represented and experienced in fellowship in the local church — right here, with these people, with whom we have covenanted together to serve the Lord. Paul says something similar in Ephesians 4:1–6:

> *"I, therefore, the prisoner of the Lord, beseech you to walk worthy of the calling with which you were called, with all lowliness and gentleness, with longsuffering, bearing with one another in love, endeavoring to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace. There is one body and one Spirit, just as you were called in one hope of your calling; one Lord, one faith, one baptism; one God and Father of all, who is above all, and through all, and in you all."*

Seven "ones" — with the middle one being that we have one Lord, Jesus Christ, the God-man. In a very real sense, fellowship with the Father and the Son is experienced in our fellowship with one another. That is the point John makes throughout: you say you love God, but you hate your brother? You cannot experience God apart from God's people.

What is fellowship? Fellowship is a shared life. Simon Kistemaker says, *"When people have fellowship, they share their mutual gifts, goals, and goods."* This is how Acts 4:32 describes the apostolic church:

> *"Now the multitude of those who believed were of one heart and one soul; neither did anyone say that any of the things he possessed was his own, but they had all things in common. And with great power the apostles gave witness to the resurrection of the Lord Jesus. And great grace was upon them all."*

The fellowship was their shared life. They lived life together.

John Stott says: *"We cannot be content with an evangelism which does not lead to the drawing of converts into the church, nor a church life whose principal cohesion is superficial camaraderie instead of spiritual fellowship with the Father and with His Son Jesus Christ."*

And Kistemaker warns us: *"How can believers be content with that which disrupts their fellowship?"* When I hear that, my natural default is to think about what other people have done to break fellowship. But that is not where we need to begin. What have *I* done to break the fellowship of the body of Christ? What ginormous log do I have in my eye?

Fellowship is the result of faith in the divine and human person of the Lord Jesus Christ.

### Joy

Look at verse 4:

> *"And these things we write to you that your joy may be full."*

I have never met a person who said the joy thing is overrated and they would rather have sadness. That person does not exist. We are designed to enjoy God — and what is part of the word "en-*joy*"? Joy. Joy in God.

John proclaims Christ the God-man so that we may have joy. When we truly find Christ, we value him, we treasure him — and that treasuring results in joy. In Matthew 13:44, Jesus says:

> *"The kingdom of heaven is like treasure hidden in a field, which a man found and hid; and for joy over it he goes and sells all that he has and buys that field."*

That is your attitude toward Christ. You treasure him in your heart so that you have joy. Romans 15:13:

> *"Now may the God of hope fill you with all joy and peace in believing."*

It is believing in the Lord Jesus Christ that brings the joy. Our joy will not be perfect until the resurrection — that is when it will be ultimately completed, when our bodies and souls are purified and brought together and we behold Jesus as he is, and shall be like him. But through faith in the whole Christ, we begin to experience what God-given joy is in this life.

The opposite of joy is self-centeredness — that inward look: *My circumstances. What is going on in my life. What people have done for me.* Faith in Jesus Christ frees us from all of that. In fact, faith in Jesus Christ is described as death to self, and that is the beginning of joy. As Psalm 16:11 says:

> *"You will show me the path of life; in Your presence is fullness of joy; at Your right hand are pleasures forevermore."*

At the right hand of the Father is Jesus Christ. It is in him, the God-man, that we find joy.

## Conclusion

This short preface packs an enormous punch. Jesus Christ is both divine and human in one person — that is the most basic element of the Apostolic Proclamation. Faith in the whole Christ unites us to the triune God, brings us together into fellowship with each other, and fills us with joy.

We need to reckon this to be so. John does not say it might become that — he says it is. Our fellowship with one another is full of joy. We need to believe that and look at it that way, instead of thinking of it as a burden or as a group of people we do not particularly like. Because you have been united to Christ, and because of that union with Christ, you are united to one another — and that is great joy.
